column2: A comprehensive blood test that measures red cells, white cells, haemoglobin levels, haematocrit, platelets, and related indices. It is used to assess overall health, detect anaemia, infection, inflammation, bleeding disorders, and to monitor various medical conditions.
column30: Collect into EDTA anticoagulated tube; ensure proper mixing to avoid platelet clumping
column31: Sample should be properly mixed to prevent clotting. Smear may be needed for morphology and manual differential if flagged by analyser.
column32: Invert tube 8–10 times immediately after collection; avoid haemolysis and ensure correct fill volume

column2: Measures the haemoglobin concentration in blood to assess oxygen-carrying capacity and aid in evaluation of anaemia, bleeding disorders, nutritional deficiencies, or general health status.
column30: Collect into EDTA anticoagulated tube.
column31: Sample should be mixed gently to avoid clotting. Ensure tube is filled to the correct volume.
column32: Invert tube 8–10 times immediately after collection; avoid haemolysis.

column2: Measures the haemoglobin concentration in blood to assess oxygen-carrying capacity and aid in evaluation of anaemia, bleeding disorders, nutritional deficiencies, or general health status.

column2: Measures the total number of white blood cells in blood to help evaluate infection, inflammation, immune status, and haematological disorders.

column32: Invert tube 8–10 times to ensure proper mixing; avoid haemolysis.

column2: Analyses the percentage distribution of different types of white blood cells (neutrophils, lymphocytes, monocytes, eosinophils, basophils) to help diagnose infections, inflammatory conditions, allergic responses, and haematological disorders.
column30: Collect in EDTA tube; prepare peripheral smear promptly for optimal morphology
column31: Blood smear should be made soon after collection to prevent artefacts. Ensure no clots are present.
column32: Invert tube 8–10 times to mix; avoid haemolysis. Prepare smear immediately if possible.

column2: Measures the number of platelets in blood to assess bleeding risk, clotting disorders, bone marrow function, and to monitor conditions like thrombocytopenia or thrombocytosis.

column2: Measures the total number of eosinophils in the blood. Helps evaluate allergic conditions, parasitic infections, drug reactions, autoimmune diseases, and disorders causing eosinophilia.

column2: A microscopic evaluation of a blood smear used to assess red cells, white cells, and platelets. Helps diagnose anaemias, infections, haemolytic disorders, blood cell morphology abnormalities, and supports interpretation of abnormal CBC results.
column30: Collect sample in EDTA tube; prepare smear promptly to preserve cell morphology.
column31: Smear should be well-made with a feathered edge and stained appropriately (e.g., Leishman, Wright, or Giemsa stain). Avoid clotted or haemolysed samples. Delayed smear preparation may alter cell shapes and staining characteristics.
column32: Invert tube 8–10 times; prepare smear immediately after collection when possible.

column2: Determines an individual’s ABO blood group and Rh (D) factor. This test is essential for safe blood transfusions, pregnancy care, medical procedures, and emergency treatment planning.
column30: Collect in EDTA tube; proper labelling and patient identification are critical
column31: Sample must be clearly labelled to avoid mismatch errors. Testing includes forward and reverse grouping, along with Rh factor determination. Repeat testing may be required for transfusion verification.
column32: Invert tube 8–10 times immediately after collection. Avoid haemolysis.

column2: Determines compatibility between donor blood and recipient blood before transfusion. This test helps prevent transfusion reactions by identifying any antigen–antibody incompatibilities.

column32: Invert tube 8–10 times after collection. Maintain proper temperature and avoid haemolysis. Ensure all identification details match the transfusion request.

column2: Detects antibodies or complement components attached to a patient’s red blood cells. Helps diagnose autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, haemolytic disease of the newborn, transfusion reactions, and drug-induced haemolysis. A titre is performed to quantify the strength of the reaction.
column30: Collect in EDTA tube; avoid haemolysis. Accurate patient identification is essential.
column31: Red cells are washed and tested with antihuman globulin to detect antibody attachment. If positive, serial dilutions are performed to determine antibody titre. Clotted samples or incorrect anticoagulant may give false results.
column32: Invert tube 8–10 times to mix; ensure sample integrity. Prompt processing is preferred for reliable results.

column2: Detects free circulating antibodies in a patient’s serum that may react with red blood cells. Commonly used in antenatal screening, pre-transfusion testing, and in evaluating alloimmunisation. When positive, a titre determines the strength and clinical significance of the detected antibodies.
column30: Collect sample in a plain red-top tube for serum or EDTA tube if plasma is required. Avoid haemolysis. Proper patient identification is essential.
column31: Patient serum/plasma is incubated with reagent red blood cells followed by antihuman globulin to detect agglutination. If positive, serial dilutions are performed to determine the antibody titre. Incorrect sample type or haemolysis may interfere with results.
column32: Allow serum sample to clot fully if using red-top tube; centrifuge to separate serum. In EDTA tubes, invert gently 8–10 times to mix.

column2: Measures the time taken for plasma to clot via the extrinsic and common coagulation pathways. Used to evaluate bleeding/clotting disorders, monitor warfarin therapy, assess liver function, and detect vitamin K deficiency. INR standardizes PT results across laboratories.
column30: Collect in 3.2% sodium citrate tube with proper filling (correct blood-to-anticoagulant ratio).
column31: Sample must be centrifuged promptly to obtain platelet-poor plasma. Clotted, haemolysed, or underfilled tubes can produce inaccurate PT/INR results. INR is calculated using PT value and ISI (International Sensitivity Index).
column32: Invert tube 3–4 times to mix anticoagulant. Process plasma quickly to avoid deterioration of clotting factors.

column2: Measures the time taken for plasma to clot via the intrinsic and common coagulation pathways. Used to evaluate bleeding disorders, monitor unfractionated heparin therapy, and assess deficiencies or inhibitors of clotting factors.
column30: Collect in 3.2% sodium citrate tube with correct blood-to-anticoagulant ratio.
column31: Sample must be centrifuged to obtain platelet-poor plasma. Clotted, underfilled, or haemolysed samples can affect results. aPTT is sensitive to factor VIII, IX, XI, XII deficiencies and presence of inhibitors like lupus anticoagulant.
column32: Invert tube 3–4 times to mix anticoagulant. Process and test plasma promptly.

column31: Sterile fluid collection containers; EDTA tube for cell count; plain tube for chemistry; cytology vial/slide for cytology.
column32: Mix EDTA tube gently; send all samples promptly for accurate cell and cytology evaluation.

column2: Used to detect and identify bacteria or fungi from infected sites to guide appropriate antimicrobial treatment.
column30: Collect adequate sample; avoid contamination; use appropriate transport medium depending on site.
column31:
Sterile swab in transport medium

Sterile container or syringe for pus aspirate
column32: Collect sample before starting antibiotics; transport quickly to lab; indicate site of collection clearly.

column1: Culture of stool specimen for Vibrio cholerae and other common bacterial enteropathogens
column2: Used to detect Vibrio cholerae and other common bacteria causing diarrheal illnesses to guide appropriate treatment and public health measures.
column30: Collect adequate stool; avoid contamination with urine or water; transport in Cary-Blair medium if delay expected.
column31: Sterile wide-mouth stool container; rectal swabs with transport medium for V. cholerae
column32: Collect fresh stool; tightly cap container; send promptly to lab for culture on selective media (e.g., TCBS agar for V. cholerae).

column1: Organism identification and antimicrobial sensitivity for cultures- swab
column2: Used to identify microorganisms grown from clinical cultures and determine their susceptibility to antibiotics to guide targeted treatment.
column30: Primary culture must yield growth; isolated colonies used for biochemical or automated identification and susceptibility panels.
column31: Culture plates, broth media, or automated system cartridges depending on method (e.g., VITEK, Phoenix)
column32: Pure colony selection is used for identification and susceptibility testing via biochemical, manual, or automated methods.

column1: Nucleic Acid Amplification Test (NAAT) for TB
column2: Used to rapidly detect Mycobacterium tuberculosis DNA and identify rifampicin resistance, aiding early diagnosis and treatment decisions.
column30: Collect adequate sample; avoid contamination; send promptly for NAAT processing (e.g., GeneXpert/CBNAAT)
column31: Sterile, leak-proof container suitable for molecular testing
column32: Collect aseptically; close tightly; send immediately to lab; avoid using fixatives (they inhibit nucleic acid tests)

column1: Early Infant Diagnostic test for HIV - Qualitative HIV-1 DNA PCR (ICTC) (Venipuncture)
column2: Used to detect HIV-1 infection in infants below 18 months by identifying proviral DNA, as maternal antibodies can interfere with antibody tests.
column30: Collect adequate blood spots on DBS card; allow to air-dry; avoid contamination and moisture; store in zip-lock bag with desiccant.
column31: DBS filter paper card; if whole blood is used, EDTA tube
column32: Collect blood aseptically; fill DBS circles completely; air-dry horizontally; package with desiccant and send to designated HIV reference lab.

column1: Early Infant Diagnostic test for HIV - Qualitative HIV-1 DNA PCR (ICTC) (Heel Prick Test)
column2: Used to detect HIV-1 infection in infants below 18 months by identifying proviral DNA, as maternal antibodies can interfere with antibody tests.
column30: Collect adequate blood spots on DBS card; allow to air-dry; avoid contamination and moisture; store in zip-lock bag with desiccant.
column31: DBS filter paper card; if whole blood is used, EDTA tube
column32: Collect blood aseptically; fill DBS circles completely; air-dry horizontally; package with desiccant and send to designated HIV reference lab.

column1: Early Infant Diagnostic test for HIV - Qualitative HIV-1 DNA PCR (ICTC)(Dried blood spot specimen)
column2: Used to detect HIV-1 infection in infants below 18 months by identifying proviral DNA, as maternal antibodies can interfere with antibody tests.
column6: Collection of dried blood spot specimen
column30: Collect adequate blood spots on DBS card; allow to air-dry; avoid contamination and moisture; store in zip-lock bag with desiccant.
column31: DBS filter paper card; if whole blood is used, EDTA tube
column32: Collect blood aseptically; fill DBS circles completely; air-dry horizontally; package with desiccant and send to designated HIV reference lab.

column2: Measures the rate at which red blood cells settle in a vertical tube over one hour. Used as a non-specific marker to detect inflammation, infection, autoimmune conditions, and to monitor disease activity.
column30: Collect blood in an EDTA tube, then transfer to a Westergren ESR tube if required by laboratory procedure.
column31: Avoid haemolysis or underfilling the tube, as these can affect sedimentation rate. Ensure tube remains vertical during testing.
column32: Invert tube 8–10 times to mix. Start ESR test promptly after collection for best results.

column2: Quantitative measurement of lipase enzyme activity in serum. Used in the diagnosis and monitoring of acute pancreatitis.
column30: Collect adequate sample; avoid hemolysis in serum sample.
column31: Serum separator tube (SST) or red-top tube for blood; sterile container for serum.
column32: Allow serum to clot at room temperature; centrifuge and separate serum within 2 hours of collection; refrigerate.

column2: Measures the concentration of urea nitrogen in blood. Used to assess renal function, hydration status, and protein metabolism.
column30: Collect adequate sample; avoid hemolysis; separate serum/plasma within 2 hours.
column31: Serum separator tube (SST) or plasma separator tube (PST) for blood.
column32: Allow serum to clot at room temperature; centrifuge and separate serum/plasma ASAP or within 2 hours; refrigerate.

column2: Semi-quantitative detection of glucose in urine using reagent strips. Commonly used for rapid screening of diabetes mellitus.
column30: Collect fresh urine sample; test immediately for accurate results.
column31: Sterile urine container.
column32: Dip test strip in fresh urine and read results according to manufacturer instructions; immediate testing preferred.

column2: Calculated measurement of circulating neutrophils in blood. Used to assess infection risk and bone marrow function.
column30: Collect adequate blood sample; mix gently by inversion; avoid clotting.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube for blood.
column32: Mix sample gently by inversion 8-10 times immediately after collection; do not refrigerate; analyze within 4-6 hours.

column2: Measures variation in red blood cell size. Used in classification and evaluation of anemia.
column30: Collect adequate blood sample; mix gently by inversion; avoid clotting.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube for blood.
column32: Mix sample gently by inversion 8-10 times immediately after collection; analyze within 6-8 hours for best results.

column2: Reflects variability in platelet size. Used to assess platelet activation and disorders.
column30: Collect adequate blood sample; mix gently by inversion; avoid clotting and platelet clumping.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube for blood.
column32: Mix sample gently by inversion 8-10 times immediately after collection; analyze within 4 hours for accurate platelet parameters.

column2: Measures average platelet size in blood. Used to evaluate platelet production and function.
column30: Collect adequate blood sample; mix gently by inversion; avoid clotting and platelet clumping.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube for blood.
column32: Mix sample gently by inversion 8-10 times immediately after collection; analyze within 4 hours for accurate MPV results.

column2: Quantitative platelet count with microscopic assessment of platelet size and shape. Used in bleeding disorders and thrombocytopenia evaluation.
column30: Mix gently; analyze within 4 hours; do not refrigerate.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube (K2EDTA).
column32: Examine smear for platelet clumps, giant platelets, satellitism.

column2: Measures the average hemoglobin content per red blood cell. Used in anemia classification.
column30: Mix gently; avoid hemolysis; calculated from Hb and RBC count.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube (K2EDTA).
column32: MCH = Hemoglobin (g/dL) / RBC count (millions/μL) × 10.

column2: Measures the average volume of red blood cells. Used to differentiate microcytic, normocytic, and macrocytic anemia.
column30: Mix gently; MCV increases with prolonged storage; analyze within 6 hours for accuracy.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube (K2EDTA).
column32: MCV = Hematocrit (%) / RBC count (millions/μL) × 10.

column2: Measures the average concentration of hemoglobin within red blood cells. Used to classify anemia and assess red cell hemoglobinization.
column30: Mix gently; avoid hemolysis; calculated from Hb and Hct.
column31: EDTA lavender-top tube (K2EDTA).
column32: MCHC = Hemoglobin (g/dL) / Hematocrit (%) × 100.

column2: Includes tests such as PT, INR, and aPTT to evaluate blood clotting pathways. Used in bleeding disorders and anticoagulant monitoring.
column30: Correct 9:1 blood to citrate ratio; centrifuge within 1 hour; test within 4 hours per NABL.
column31: 3.2% sodium citrate tube (light blue top).
column32: Centrifuge at 1500g for 15 minutes; separate platelet-poor plasma; test within 4 hours or freeze at -20°C.

column2: Standard histopathological staining technique used to evaluate tissue architecture and cellular morphology. Forms the primary basis for histopathological diagnosis.
column30: Fix in 10% neutral buffered formalin immediately; 10:1 fixative to tissue ratio.
column31: Container with 10% neutral buffered formalin.
column32: Fix for 6-48 hours depending on size; process; embed; section at 4-5 microns; stain H&E.

column2: Fine Needle Aspiration Cytology specimen stained with May–Grünwald–Giemsa for evaluation of cellular morphology. Used in the assessment of masses, lymph nodes, and other lesions.
column30: Prepare smears immediately at site; air dry for MGG; fix in alcohol for Pap.
column31: Glass slides; needle aspirate.
column32: Air dry smears; stain with May-Grünwald-Giemsa; examine under microscopy.

column2: Application of additional cytochemical or microbiological stains on FNAC smears when clinically indicated. Used to aid diagnosis when routine staining is inconclusive.
column30: Prepare additional smears for special stains; specify stain required.
column31: Glass slides.
column32: Perform ZN stain for AFB, PAS, mucicarmine, or other stains as indicated.

column2: Ultrasound-guided FNAC for precise sampling of lesions not easily accessible clinically.
column30: Coordinate with radiology; ensure real-time visualization; prepare smears immediately.
column31: Glass slides; sterile needle aspirate.
column32: Prepare smears immediately; air dry for MGG; alcohol fix for Pap.

column2: Cytological evaluation of lavage fluid to assess infections, malignancy, or interstitial lung disease.
column30: Collect in sterile container; process promptly; aliquot for different tests.
column31: Sterile container.
column32: Cytocentrifuge; prepare smears; stain with Pap, MGG, and special stains as needed.

column2: Serological agglutination test detecting antibodies against Salmonella Typhi and Paratyphi antigens. Used in the supportive diagnosis of enteric fever.
column30: Separate serum; test for O and H antigens; interpret with clinical correlation.
column31: Serum separator tube (SST).
column32: Perform slide/tube agglutination with O and H antigens; report titers.

column2: Detects dengue virus NS1 antigen in serum during early acute infection. Useful in early diagnosis of dengue fever.
column30: Collect during first 5 days of fever; separate serum.
column31: Serum separator tube (SST).
column32: Perform ELISA or rapid immunochromatographic test for NS1 antigen.

column2: Detects IgM antibodies against dengue virus indicating recent infection.
column30: Collect after day 5 of fever; separate serum.
column31: Serum separator tube (SST).
column32: Perform ELISA for IgM antibodies; interpret with clinical history.

column2: Detects IgG antibodies against dengue virus indicating past exposure or secondary infection.
column30: Separate serum; useful for secondary infection diagnosis.
column31: Serum separator tube (SST).
column32: Perform ELISA for IgG antibodies; high IgG in acute phase suggests secondary infection.

column2: Measures concentration of Hepatitis B surface antigen in serum. Used for disease monitoring and treatment response.
column30: Separate serum; quantitative result for monitoring.
column31: Serum separator tube (SST).
column32: Perform quantitative immunoassay; report in IU/mL.

column2: Detects P. falciparum–specific antigens in blood. Used for rapid malaria diagnosis.
column30: Use fresh whole blood; perform test immediately.
column31: EDTA tube or capillary blood.
column32: Apply blood to rapid test cassette; read within specified time per kit instructions.

column2: Detects P. vivax–specific antigens in blood. Used for rapid malaria diagnosis.
column30: Use fresh whole blood; perform test immediately.
column31: EDTA tube or capillary blood.
column32: Apply blood to rapid test cassette; read within specified time per kit instructions.

column1: ZN Stain for Extra pulmonary sample
column2: Ziehl–Neelsen staining to detect acid-fast bacilli in non-sputum specimens. Used in extra-pulmonary tuberculosis diagnosis.
column30: Collect adequate specimen; process for concentration before staining.
column31: Sterile container.
column32: Concentrate specimen; prepare smear; perform ZN staining; examine for AFB.

column2: Routine microscopic examination of stool for ova, cysts, and parasites. Used in evaluation of intestinal parasitic infections.
column30: Collect fresh stool; examine within 30 minutes for trophozoites; preserve for delayed examination.
column31: Sterile stool container or SAF/PVA preservative.
column32: Direct wet mount in saline and iodine; concentration techniques; permanent stain if indicated.

column1: Sickling Test for Sickle cell anemia*
column2: A screening test that detects the presence of sickle hemoglobin by inducing red blood cell sickling under low-oxygen conditions, used for early identification of sickle cell anemia.
column30: Capillary blood (finger-prick) and a rapid test kit for point-of-care screening.
column31: Detects sickle hemoglobin by inducing red blood cell sickling, used for preliminary identification of sickle cell disease or trait.
column32: Point of care test for screening of Sickle cell anaemia

column2: Measures the blood’s capacity to bind iron with transferrin, helping assess iron deficiency and iron metabolism disorders.
column30: Serum or plasma suitable for liver enzyme analysis
column31: Serum separation tube (SST) or plain red-top tube
column32: Collect blood, allow to clot if serum, centrifuge promptly, and process as per lab protocol.
